question: Hey, I’m kinda stuck on this question and was wonderin’ if you could help me out. So the question is: What parts you need in a argumentative essay and how they work together?

answer1.answer: When you writin' a argumentative essay, you gotta start off with a clear intro sayin' what your main point is, like what you tryna argue. answer2.answer: An argumentative essay gotta got a clear thesis, sayin’ what you really tryna argue. You need some real proof—like examples, stats, or what the experts be sayin’—to back it up. You gotta bring up what folks who disagree might say, and then show why they wrong, so you show you understand what you talkin’ 'bout for real. The conclusion just sum up your main points and put your thesis in a new way, so it stick with folks. question: Hey, I'm stuck on this question real quick, was wonderin' if you could help me out. The question be like: How the elements in the Periodic Table relate to each other and how they different?
prompt: How do the elements in the Periodic Table relate and differ from each other? Your answer MUST NOT contain rich text. Your answer should be within 150 words.

answer1.answer:
Elements on the periodic table, they go up by atomic number, you know, that’s just how many protons they got in they nucleus. Elements in the same group—like in the same column—gon’ got similar chemical stuff goin’ on ’cause they got the same number of valence electrons, that’s the ones that be doin’ the bondin’. Same period, like in the same row, they got the same number of electron shells.

But what make ‘em different is how many protons, neutrons, and electrons they got. That’s what switch up the size, how strong they pullin’ on electrons (electronegativity), how hard it is to snatch a electron from ‘em (ionization energy), and whether they actin’ like metals or not. Like, metals be losin’ electrons real easy, but nonmetals be tryna grab ‘em. So that’s why the periodic table set up like that—it be showin’ how all these patterns and differences keep poppin' up with the elements.

answer2.answer:
Elements in the Periodic Table be lined up by how many protons they got, so the atomic number just keep goin' up. Elements in the same group (go straight down) be havin' the same kinda chemical things goin' on, 'cause they got the same number of valence electrons, and that’s what be tryna tell you how they act. Elements in the same period (across the row) be on the same energy level, so they showin' them trends in how big they is and how much energy it take to pull off an electron.

What make ‘em different be how many protons, neutrons, and electrons they got. That change up they size, mass, how they pull on other things, and how wild they get. Metals usually be losin' they electrons easy, but nonmetals be pickin' up electrons instead. Metalloids? They somewhere between the two. The periodic table just set all that up, so you can peep the patterns and know how elements gon' act.

question: Hey, I’m stuck on this question real quick, was wonderin’ if you could help me out. So the question is: What the main parts of a full sentence and how they go together?

question: Hey, I’m kinda stuck on this question and was wonderin’ if you could help me out. Here go the question: How the water cycle all connect, and why every stage matter like that?

answer1.answer:

All that water gettin' collected up in them rivers, lakes, and the ocean, just to go back and start all over with that evaporation.

Every part be real important: Evaporation be what make the water vapor for the clouds; condensation makin’ the clouds that drop that rain or snow; precipitation be puttin' the water back in the rivers and lakes so we got fresh water; and collection make sure water’s still out here for everybody and everything. All these parts be workin' together to keep the water on Earth balanced, holdin' it down for all the life and ecosystems out here.

answer2.answer: The water cycle always be goin’. Evaporation, that part where the sun heatin’ water up and turn it into vapor, that’s how water get up off the ground and head to the sky. Collection be gatherin’ all that water in the rivers, lakes, and oceans, puttin’ it back where it belong. Transpiration, that’s just plants lettin’ water vapor out, addin’ to all that moisture in the air. Every step matter—evaporation get it started, condensation make them clouds and that rain, precipitation bringin’ back the water, and collection keepin’ us and everything else goin’. All of it connect together, so the water always movin’, holdin’ up the whole ecosystem and keepin’ life goin’.

question: Hey, I’m kinda stuck on this question and was wonderin’ if you could help me out. The question be: How you use the Pythagorean theorem to find the length of the other side of a triangle?
prompt: How would you use the Pythagorean theorem to find the length of the third side of a triangle? Your answer MUST NOT contain rich text. Your answer should be within 150 words.

answer1.answer:
The Pythagorean theorem only work for right-angle triangles. It say the longest side, the hypotenuse—the one across from the right angle—if you square that, it’s the same as addin’ up the squares of the other two sides, the legs.

If you tryna figure out how long one of the sides is:

1. First, you gotta know which sides you got. Call ‘em a and b. If you got the hypotenuse, that’s c.

2. Then, you use the theorem. If you got a and b, c² = a² + b², so c just gon’ be the square root of that. If you got a and c, or b and c, just flip it around: a² = c² - b² or b² = c² - a², then take the square root.

3. Do the math to get your missing side. Just make sure your answer a positive number, since it’s a length.

answer2.answer:
The Pythagorean theorem only work for them right-angle triangles. Basically, it say the square of that hypotenuse (that longest side across from the right angle) equal to the sum of the squares of them other two sides — them what folks call the legs or whatever.

If you tryna figure out one side, you gotta know the other two. Let’s call the sides a, b, and c, with c bein’ the hypotenuse.

If you know a and b, you use: c² = a² + b². Then you just take the square root of c² to get c.

If you know a and c, you use: b² = c² - a². Then you find the square root of b² to get b.

If you know b and c, you do: a² = c² - b². Then you find the square root of a² to get a. Remember, always take the positive square root, 'cause length can’t be negative.

answer2.answer: The order you do math in, that’s what folks be rememberin’ as PEMDAS — you know, Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication and Division, Addin' and Subtractin'. First, you gotta handle what’s in them parentheses. Then you deal with the exponents. After that, you multiply and divide goin’ left to right, ‘cause they both on the same level. Last, you add and subtract, still movin’ left to right, ‘cause them got equal rank too. When you followin’ it like that every time, you only gon’ get one right answer, no matter how wild that equation lookin’. But if you don’t do it in that order, you prolly gon’ mess around and get it wrong.

question: Hey, I’m kinda stuck on this question and was wonderin' if you could help me out real quick. So the question go like: How you use supply and demand to explain why somethin' price might go up?

question: Hey, I’m kinda stuck on this question and was wonderin’ if you could help me out. The question be: Usin’ the laws of motion, how you explain why seat belts be necessary in cars?
prompt: Using the laws of motion, how would you explain why seat belts are necessary in cars? Your answer MUST NOT contain rich text. Your answer should be within 150 words.

answer1.answer: Newton first law say if somethin' movin', it gon' keep movin' same speed, same way, 'less some unbalanced force stop it. So when you got a sudden stop, like a car crash, the car be hittin' them brakes real hard, that’s that big unbalanced force. If you ain’t got no seatbelt, your body still movin' like the car was before. That end up makin' you hit the inside of the car, which could be real bad, maybe even deadly. The seatbelt be that unbalanced force you need, slowin' you down with the car so you don't get thrown forward or take a hard hit. That’s why seatbelts real important for stoppin' folks from gettin' hurt in crashes, 'cause they fight against that inertia.

answer2.answer:
Newton first law be sayin' if somethin' already movin', it gon' keep movin' same speed, same direction, unless somethin' else come mess it up. So, if you in a car and it stop all of a sudden, like in a crash, yo body still tryna keep goin’ at how fast the car was goin’. If you ain’t got no seatbelt on, that inertia gon’ got you flyin’ forward, which can mess you up bad or even kill you, 'cause you gon’ hit somethin’ or get thrown out the car.

A seatbelt that thing that slow you down with the car, bringin’ you to a stop more easy. It keep you from turnin’ into a projectile, so you ain’t gettin' hit as hard and you less likely to get hurt. That’s why seatbelts real important when it come to collisions—they protect you from what Newton law be talkin’ 'bout, keepin’ you safe from all that danger.

question: Hey, I’m stuck on this question and was wonderin’ if you could help me out. The question go like: With what you know ’bout the condensed water cycle, how would you use it to say why a glass with a cold drink get wet on the outside when it’s hot out?

answer1.answer: On a hot day, the air got hella water vapor in it. When you bring out a cold glass, the glass be colder than the air dew point. Basically, the cold glass just be actin' like a spot for all that air moisture to condense on, kinda like how water cycle be workin', just on the low.

answer2.answer: On a hot day, the air be holdin' hella water vapor. When you bring out a cold glass, the outside be colder than the dew point in the air.
